original_hypothesis.title: Token Owner Resets Active Genesis Pool via Re-deposit Locking User Funds
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: A whitelisted token owner can call depositNativeToken again after the pool reaches PRE_LISTING or PRE_LAUNCH with user deposits. setGenesisPoolInfo resets poolStatus to NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED and zeros allocatedFundingAmount/allocatedNativeAmount, while existing userDeposits and totalDeposits remain unchanged. User funding tokens become permanently locked since claimDeposits requires NOT_QUALIFIED status.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: setGenesisPoolInfo has no pool status guard - it unconditionally sets poolStatus = NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED. The manager's depositNativeToken reuses an existing pool if getGenesisPool returns non-zero. After approval (PRE_LISTING), getGenesisPool returns the pool, so setGenesisPoolInfo is called on a pool with active user deposits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setGenesisPoolInfo unconditionally sets poolStatus = NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED and resets allocations without checking current status or clearing userDeposits

preconditions: Token owner is whitelisted for the native token
Pool has reached PRE_LISTING or PRE_LAUNCH with user deposits
Token owner has additional native tokens to transfer
Pair has zero balances (holds before launch)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: setGenesisPoolInfo resets allocatedFundingAmount=0, allocatedNativeAmount=0 but does NOT reset userDeposits or totalDeposits
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Token owner calls depositNativeToken again; existing user funding tokens locked in pool with status NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED; claimDeposits requires NOT_QUALIFIED
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Token owner locks depositors' funding tokens indefinitely, can repeatedly reset to extract new deposits

economic_questions: Can the token owner extract the locked funding tokens somehow?
Can governance rescue locked funds by setting status to NOT_QUALIFIED?
Is the whitelist persistent enough for repeated exploitation?
validation_requirements: Trace depositNativeToken → setGenesisPoolInfo path on active pool
Verify userDeposits are NOT reset by setGenesisPoolInfo

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Native tokens to transfer (cost depends on token value)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All deposited funding tokens locked (100% of deposits at risk)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on funding token value in pool

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Token owner needs native tokens for re-deposit
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After maximum user deposits, before launch
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of deposited funding tokens for users

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All depositors in the genesis pool

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify pair balance checks pass for re-deposit scenario

validation_result.mechanism: State reset without clearing user deposits
validation_result.impact_summary: A whitelisted token owner can call depositNativeToken on an active pool (PRE_LISTING/PRE_LAUNCH), which invokes setGenesisPoolInfo that resets poolStatus to NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED and zeros allocation tracking, while userDeposits and totalDeposits remain non-zero, creating an inconsistent state that locks user funding tokens.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability stems from two interacting code paths. First, GenesisPoolFactory.getGenesisPool() returns a non-zero address for any pool whose status is not NOT_QUALIFIED, meaning pools in PRE_LISTING or PRE_LAUNCH status are returned. Second, GenesisPoolManager.depositNativeToken() reuses an existing pool when getGenesisPool returns non-zero, and unconditionally calls setGenesisPoolInfo() on it. This function has no status guard - it resets allocatedFundingAmount and allocatedNativeAmount to 0 and sets poolStatus to NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED, but leaves userDeposits mapping, totalDeposits, depositers array, and the actual funding token balance completely untouched. After this reset: (1) claimDeposits() reverts because it requires NOT_QUALIFIED status; (2) depositToken() reverts because it requires PRE_LISTING or PRE_LAUNCH; (3) the accounting is corrupted because totalDeposits reflects old deposits while allocatedFundingAmount is 0. While governance CAN rescue by calling rejectGenesisPool (which works from NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED status), this does not negate the code defect - the missing status guard in setGenesisPoolInfo is a clear logic error.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code evidence at GenesisPool.sol lines 67-88 confirms no status check before resetting pool state. GenesisPoolFactory.sol lines 76-86 confirm getGenesisPool returns active pools. GenesisPoolManager.sol lines 97-128 confirm depositNativeToken reuses existing pools and calls setGenesisPoolInfo unconditionally.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No status check (e.g., require(poolStatus == PoolStatus.DEFAULT || poolStatus == PoolStatus.NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED)) before resetting allocations and pool status. Does not clear userDeposits, totalDeposits, or depositers array when resetting allocatedFundingAmount and allocatedNativeAmount to 0.

Returns the existing pool for any status other than NOT_QUALIFIED (including PRE_LISTING and PRE_LAUNCH with active user deposits), enabling depositNativeToken to operate on pools with existing depositors.

When getGenesisPool returns a non-zero pool (active pool in PRE_LISTING/PRE_LAUNCH), it proceeds to call setGenesisPoolInfo on it without checking if the pool has active user deposits, triggering the state reset.

calling_sequence: 1. Token owner (whitelisted) calls GenesisPoolManager.depositNativeToken(nativeToken, ...) on a token whose pool is in PRE_LISTING with user deposits
2. genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(nativeToken) returns the existing pool (non-zero because status != NOT_QUALIFIED) at GenesisPoolFactory.sol:76-86
3. Since genesisPool != address(0), createGenesisPool is skipped
4. Native tokens are transferred to the pool via safeTransferFrom
6. setGenesisPoolInfo at GenesisPool.sol:67-88 resets allocatedFundingAmount=0, allocatedNativeAmount=0, poolStatus=NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED
7. userDeposits mapping, totalDeposits, and actual funding token balance are NOT reset
8. Users cannot call claimDeposits() because poolStatus is NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED (not NOT_QUALIFIED)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path starts at GenesisPoolManager.depositNativeToken (line 97), which calls GenesisPoolFactory.getGenesisPool (line 115-116). When the pool is in PRE_LISTING/PRE_LAUNCH, the factory returns the existing pool address. The manager then calls setGenesisPoolInfo (line 127) on this active pool. Inside GenesisPool.setGenesisPoolInfo (line 67), the function unconditionally resets allocationInfo.allocatedNativeAmount=0 (line 76), allocationInfo.allocatedFundingAmount=0 (line 77), and sets poolStatus=NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED (line 81). The totalDeposits variable (line 37) and userDeposits mapping (line 36) are never touched. The claimDeposits function (line 208) requires poolStatus == NOT_QUALIFIED, so users cannot withdraw their funding tokens.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for genesis pool contracts that would affect this vulnerability.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: 1. Token owner gets whitelisted for nativeToken and calls depositNativeToken, creating a pool. 2. Governance approves the pool (approveGenesisPool), setting status to PRE_LISTING. 3. Users deposit funding tokens via depositToken, increasing totalDeposits and userDeposits. 4. Token owner calls depositNativeToken again with the same nativeToken. The pair exists but has zero balances (no launch yet), so pair balance checks pass. getGenesisPool returns the existing pool (status PRE_LISTING != NOT_QUALIFIED). setGenesisPoolInfo resets allocations to 0 and status to NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED. 5. User funding tokens are now stuck: claimDeposits requires NOT_QUALIFIED, depositToken requires PRE_LISTING/PRE_LAUNCH. Governance intervention is needed to reject the pool to unlock funds.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. A whitelisted token owner can unilaterally reset an active genesis pool's accounting state while user deposits remain locked. This creates: (1) immediate fund locking for all depositors until governance intervenes, (2) accounting corruption where totalDeposits > 0 but allocatedFundingAmount = 0, (3) if the pool is re-approved and progresses, the pre-existing totalDeposits will cause incorrect allocation calculations in FixedAuction.getNativeTokenAmount since it uses proposedNativeAmount/proposedFundingAmount ratios against the stale totalDeposits. While governance can rescue via rejectGenesisPool, the defect enables a token owner to repeatedly grief depositors and create persistent accounting inconsistencies.
recommended_mitigations: Add a status guard to setGenesisPoolInfo: require(poolStatus == PoolStatus.DEFAULT, "Pool already initialized");
Alternatively, if re-initialization is intended, clear all user-facing state: reset totalDeposits to 0, clear the depositers array, and refund or reset all userDeposits entries
Consider creating a new pool via createGenesisPool instead of reusing existing pools with active deposits
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all genesis pool contracts (factory, manager, auction factory, fixed auction). 2. Whitelist token owner for a native token. 3. Call depositNativeToken to create pool. 4. Call approveGenesisPool to move to PRE_LISTING. 5. Have multiple users call depositToken to accumulate totalDeposits > 0. 6. Token owner calls depositNativeToken again with same nativeToken. 7. Verify poolStatus is NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED, allocatedFundingAmount is 0, but totalDeposits > 0 and userDeposits are intact. 8. Verify users' claimDeposits() reverts with 'INS'. 9. Verify accounting corruption by checking _getNativeTokenAmount(totalDeposits) against new proposedNativeAmount.
uncertainties: Whether the whitelist persists across re-deposits (it appears to be a persistent mapping, so yes)
Whether pair balance checks might fail in edge cases where pair was seeded with tokens between approval and re-deposit
additional_testing_needed: Test the full scenario end-to-end in a fork/local environment
Test whether governance rejection properly recovers all user funds after the reset
Test accounting impact if pool is re-approved after reset with stale totalDeposits
